mission.title: Install and Configure Backstage on Kubernetes
mission.description: Backstage is an open framework for building developer portals that helps organizations manage their software catalog and streamline development processes. Installing Backstage allows teams to unify their infrastructure tooling and enhance productivity.

steps.title: Clone the Backstage repository
steps.description:
Start by cloning the Backstage repository to your local machine. This will allow you to customize your Backstage instance as needed.
```bash
git clone https://github.com/backstage/backstage.git
cd backstage
```

steps.title: Install dependencies
steps.description:
Install the necessary dependencies using Yarn. This step ensures that all required packages are available for your Backstage instance.
```bash
yarn install
```

steps.title: Build the Backstage application
steps.description:
Build the Backstage application to prepare it for deployment. This step compiles the application and prepares it for running in a Kubernetes environment.
```bash
yarn build
```

steps.title: Deploy Backstage to Kubernetes
steps.description:
Apply the Kubernetes manifests to deploy Backstage. Make sure to replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace.
```bash
kubectl apply -f k8s/ --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the deployment
steps.description:
Check the status of the Backstage pods to ensure they are running correctly. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the deployment to set resource limits for the Backstage pods. This helps ensure that your application runs efficiently within your Kubernetes cluster.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment backstage --namespace <namespace>
# Add resource limits under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
```

uninstall.title: Delete the Backstage deployment
uninstall.description:
Remove the Backstage deployment from your Kubernetes cluster. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete deployment backstage --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up associated resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any associated services and persistent volume claims (PVCs) created during the installation. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete service backstage --namespace <namespace>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
If you created a new namespace for Backstage, you can delete it to clean up your Kubernetes environment. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up your current Backstage configuration and data. This ensures you can restore your system if needed.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace <namespace> -o yaml > backup-backstage.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Pull the latest changes
upgrade.description:
Fetch the latest changes from the Backstage repository to get the newest version.
```bash
git pull origin main
```

upgrade.title: Rebuild the Backstage application
upgrade.description:
Rebuild the application to ensure all new changes are compiled and ready for deployment.
```bash
yarn build
```

upgrade.title: Redeploy Backstage
upgrade.description:
Apply the updated Kubernetes manifests to redeploy Backstage. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl apply -f k8s/ --namespace <namespace>
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Backstage pods to ensure they are running the new version correctly. Replace `<namespace>` with your chosen namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending state
troubleshooting.description:
If your Backstage pods are stuck in Pending, check for insufficient resources in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Backstage not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access Backstage, ensure that the service is correctly configured and the port is exposed.
```bash
kubectl get service --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Error in application logs
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter errors in the application, check the logs for more details.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Backstage will have all pods running without errors, and you will be able to access the Backstage interface through the configured service. Ensure that resource limits are set appropriately for optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
kubectl edit deployment backstage --namespace <namespace>
# Add resource limits under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and the necessary tools installed on your local machine.
